coredata.dc:title: Copper doped carbon dots for selective determination of regulated aniline additives and safe hair dyeing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zhanghong
Zhou, Lin
Zhang, Liping
Cui, Haining
Ma, Jinxin
Wang, Chan
Song, Qijun
coredata.dc:description:
Background Hair dyes, as common daily chemical products, have been integrated into human daily life. However, the aniline additives contained in traditional hair dyes pose potential threats to health. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop an eco-friendly and convenient detection method and to explore safe alternatives. In this context, enzymatic catalysis technology has received widespread attention due to its safety and eco-friendliness.
Results Herein, copper-doped carbon dots (CuCDs) with laccase-like activity were prepared by a one-step calcination of copper sulfate, amaranth and ammonium chloride. The resultant CuCDs are monodispersed ellipsoidal crystals with an average diameter of 3.4 nm and active copper centers similar to that of natural laccase. Enzyme kinetics experiments demonstrated that the maximum rate constant (Vmax) obtained from CuCDs is 59 times greater than that of natural laccase, and the Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) is only about half that of natural laccase, indicating a high affinity for laccase substrate. The CuCDs also exhibited a superior stability in comparison with the natural laccase, as over 90 % of their catalytic activity can be maintained in wide pH, temperatures and more than 30 days storage. In the presence of oxygen, CuCDs can not only catalyze the chromogenic reaction of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD), allowing for the selective and rapid quantification of these aniline additives in hair dye products, but also catalyze the polymerization of dopamine (DA) under ambient conditions for hair dyeing.
Significance This work not only provides an effective method for the determination of aniline additives in hair dyes, but also offers new approaches for safe hair dyeing, and expands the application of carbon nanomaterials in the cosmetics field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper doped carbon dots
Laccase-like activity
O-phenylenediamine
P-phenylenediamine
Hair dyeing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability assessment of laccase-like activity of CuCDs
The determination of PPD and OPD in hair dyes
Synthesis and characterization of CuCDs nanozyme
Laccase-like activity and catalytic stability of CuCDs
The CuCDs catalyzed DA polymerization for hair dyeing

We would like to acknowledge the work of Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of CuCDs. (A) TEM (Inset: size distribution and high resolution TEM) image. (B) AFM image (inset: the height profile of CuCDs measured along the line shown). (C) XRD pattern. (D) UV–vis absorbance spectra for the CDs, CuCDs and amaranth. (E) FT-IR spectra of amaranth, CuCDs, CDs. (F) XPS spectrum of CuCDs and (G–H) high-resolution XPS spectra of Cu 2p and Cu LMM.

Study of the laccase-like activity. (A) Schematic illustration of the laccase-like catalytic reaction with 2,4-DP and 4-AP. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of the reaction systems. (C) The absorbance at 505 nm of the reaction catalyzed by CuCDs under different pH (D) Lineweaver-Burk plot of laccase-like activity of the CuCDs versus varying concentration of 2,4-DP. (Inset: Double reciprocal plots of laccase-like activity of CuCDs.) (E) Comparison of K m, Vmax and K cat/K m in this work with other literature [9,34–37].

Catalytic stability study of CuCDs. Relative catalytic activity of laccase and CuCDs at the same mass concentration of 0.1 mg/mL under different (A) pH, (B) temperature, (C) storage time, (D) 100 mM inorganic salts, (E) 0.5 % (w/w) surfactants, (F) 100 mM organic solvents.

UV–vis absorption spectra of 0.1 mg⋅mL−1 CuCDs reaction system with (A) PPD and (B) OPD (inset: the actual color changes). (C–D) Linear plot for the determination of PPD and OPD, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
CuCDs catalyze the oxidation of (A) PPD and (B) OPD to Bandrowski's base and 2,3-diaminophenazine, respectively.

UV–vis spectra of PDA solutions obtained under different (A) DA concentrations, (B) pH, (C) atmospheres, (D) temperatures, (E) SDBS concentrations. (F) Photographs of hair samples before and after treatment with (a) 100 μg/mL CuCDs + 5 mg/mL DA + 7 mg/mL SDBS; (b) 10 mg/mL laccase + 5 mg/mL DA; (c) 10 mM CuSO4 + 15 mM H2O2 + 5 mg/mL DA; (d) 10 mM CuSO4 + 5 mg/mL DA; (e) 15 mM H2O2 + 5 mg/mL DA; (f) 3 % NH4OH + 5 mg/mL DA. (G) The corresponding L∗ values.

The cytotoxicity study of CuCDs. The CCK-8 viability analysis of HaCaT cells after incubated for (A) 8 h and (B) 20 h. (C) Live/dead staining of HaCaT cells after incubation with a: blank, b: 0.1 μg/mL, c: 1 μg/mL, d: 5 μg/mL of CuCDs for 20 h. The CCK-8 viability analysis of HDF cells after incubated for (D) 8 h and (E) 20 h. (F) Live/dead staining of HDF cells after incubation with a: blank, b: 0.1 μg/mL, c: 1 μg/mL, d: 5 μg/mL of CuCDs for 20 h. The values are expressed as the means ± standard deviation of triplicates. ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01 and ∗∗∗P < 0.001.
The proposed reaction routes for the CuCDs-catalyzed DA polymerization.
Determination of PPD and OPD in actual dyed hair samples.

head.ce:title: Copper doped carbon dots for selective determination of regulated aniline additives and safe hair dyeing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hair dyes, as common daily chemical products, have been integrated into human daily life. However, the aniline additives contained in traditional hair dyes pose potential threats to health. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop an eco-friendly and convenient detection method and to explore safe alternatives. In this context, enzymatic catalysis technology has received widespread attention due to its safety and eco-friendliness.

Herein, copper-doped carbon dots (CuCDs) with laccase-like activity were prepared by a one-step calcination of copper sulfate, amaranth and ammonium chloride. The resultant CuCDs are monodispersed ellipsoidal crystals with an average diameter of 3.4 nm and active copper centers similar to that of natural laccase. Enzyme kinetics experiments demonstrated that the maximum rate constant (Vmax) obtained from CuCDs is 59 times greater than that of natural laccase, and the Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) is only about half that of natural laccase, indicating a high affinity for laccase substrate. The CuCDs also exhibited a superior stability in comparison with the natural laccase, as over 90 % of their catalytic activity can be maintained in wide pH, temperatures and more than 30 days storage. In the presence of oxygen, CuCDs can not only catalyze the chromogenic reaction of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD), allowing for the selective and rapid quantification of these aniline additives in hair dye products, but also catalyze the polymerization of dopamine (DA) under ambient conditions for hair dyeing.

This work not only provides an effective method for the determination of aniline additives in hair dyes, but also offers new approaches for safe hair dyeing, and expands the application of carbon nanomaterials in the cosmetics field.

Image 1
Copper doped carbon dots (CuCDs) obtained from calcination of amaranth and copper sulfate exhibited an excellent laccase-like activity, which were successfully applied for the rapid and selective determination of regulated aniline additives and safe hair dyeing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increased health and environment concerns, the potential risks of conventional hair dyes have attracted a great deal of public attention. Many hair dye formulations contain ingredients such as o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) [1]. They are typically aniline derivatives, and prolonged exposure to these chemicals could pose a great threat to human health [2,3]. In particular, OPD, a commonly used dye precursor, has been identified as a potent allergen that can cause contact dermatitis, redness, itching, and other related symptoms [4]. Due to the potential harm of these aniline derivatives, their addition to cosmetic products is strictly regulated in many countries. For example, the Cosmetics Regulation of the European Union currently permits PPD in hair dye products with a maximum concentration limit of 6 % and prohibits the addition of OPD [5]. With the rising concerns to the safety of OPD and PPD in hair dyes, there is an increasing demand for the monitoring of aniline derivatives.

Many methods have been reported to determine PPD and OPD, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], fluorescence [7], and electrochemistry [8], but these methods require complex instruments and prolonged analysis time. Compared to these methods, colorimetric methods are more convenient for field-based rapid detection due to their simple operation, low cost, and high efficiency [9]. Especially laccase-based colorimetric detection methods have gained attention in recent years [10]. As a natural oxidoreductase, laccase can catalyze the oxidation of aniline compounds and produce various colored products that can be exploited for determination of the harmful components in hair dyes [9].

To avoid the use of these potentially hazardous chemicals, the development of eco-friendly alternatives is urgently required. In this context, DA has gained attention as an alternative to those toxic colorants in hair dyes because it can be oxidized to form polydopamine (PDA), which is similar to natural melanin in composition and structure [11]. To promote the oxidative polymerization of DA, oxidants or alkaline medium, such as H2O2, NaOH or NH3OH are usually required [12]. The strong alkaline and oxidants can cause an irreversible damage to hairs. In contrast, laccase catalyzed polymerization of DA could proceed in a mild condition, offering a safe and eco-friendly hair dyeing method.

Natural laccase is a water-soluble copper blue oxidase, which can effectively catalyze the oxidation of phenols, aromatic amines and other substrates in the presence of molecular oxygen [13]. The catalytic center of laccase consists of nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur coordinated copper ions [14]. As a natural protein, however, the activity of laccase is affected by many factors, such as pH, temperature and storage time, which strongly limited its wide applications [15]. Carbon dots not only possess the stability of nanomaterials and a wide range of precursor sources but also have demonstrated a great potential in the fields of catalysis, sensors, and biomedicine sources [16–18]. In this study, copper-doped carbon dots (CuCDs) nanozyme was synthesized by one-step calcination with copper sulfate as metal source and amaranth as carbon source. Amaranth is selected as the precursor because it is a water-soluble food color additive with low toxicity, and the azo group, oxygen and sulfur atom in its structure can readily coordinate with copper ions, thus promoting the doping of copper ions. Consequently, the as-synthesized CuCDs exhibit higher laccase activity compared to natural laccase and other reported nanozymes [19,20], which can rapidly catalyze the chromogenic reaction of OPD and PPD. Other components in hair dye had no interference with the chromogenic reaction, which facilitates the selective detection of OPD and PPD. Moreover, the CuCDs can catalyze DA polymerization under ambient conditions, avoiding the use of strong oxidants and bases during the hair dyeing process, thus providing an eco-friendly method for hair dyeing. This work presents a promising solution for both the determination of PPD and OPD and safe hair dyeing, expanding the application of nanozymes in the cosmetic fields.

Natural laccase (10000 U/g) was bought from Aladdin Chemicals Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Grey hair was obtained from China elderly volunteers. All Chemicals were of analytical grade and purchased from Energy Chemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

The CuCDs were prepared by one-step calcination method. Briefly, 80 mg of amaranth, 20 mg of copper sulfate and 80 mg of NH4Cl were ground in a mortar. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and calcined at 200 °C for 4 h. The calcined product was transferred to water and centrifuged to remove large particles, and the obtained supernatant was dialysed for 12 h with a 1000 Da dialysis bag. Finally, the resulting CuCDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying. For comparison, the CDs without copper doping were synthesized using the same method as that for CuCDs preparation.

The chromogenic reaction of 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DP) with 4-aminoantipyrine (4-AP) was used to evaluate the laccase-like activity of the CuCDs. The reaction was conducted in a mixture containing 700 μL of 0.1 M HEPES buffer (pH 7.0), 100 μL of 1 mg/mL 2,4-DP solution, 100 μL of 1 mg/mL 4-AP solution, and 100 μL of 1 mg/mL CuCDs solution. The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 20 min and the corresponding absorbances were measured at 505 nm.

To determine the kinetic parameters, laccase-like activity assays were performed at 30 °C, by ranging the 2,4-DP concentration from 0.1 to 0.8 mM. The experimental data were fitted to the modified Michaelis-Menten equation (1) [21].
(1)
1 V
=
K m
V max
×
1 c
+
1
V max
Where V is the initial reaction rate, c is the substrate concentration, V max and K
m are the maximum velocity and the Michaelis-Menten constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability assessment of laccase-like activity of CuCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic stability of the CuCDs and laccase was evaluated by catalyzing the chromogenic reaction of 2,4-DP with 4-AP. The effect of pH on their catalytic activity was investigated at room temperature with a pH range of 3.0–9.0. Their thermal stability was assessed from 0 to 60 °C at pH 7.0. To evaluate storage stability, these catalysts were stored at room temperature, and their catalytic activity was measured every 3–5 days. To explore the influence of various inorganic salts on catalytic performance, 100 μL aliquots of 100 mM solutions of NaF, NaCl, KCl, Na2SO4, and Na2CO3 were added to the reaction mixture, respectively. Additionally, to assess the impact of different ionic surfactants, 100 μL of 0.5 % (w/w) solutions of sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS), cocamidopropyl betaine (CAB), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), and dodecyltrimethylammonium chloride (DTAC) were introduced into the reaction mixture, respectively. The stability of these materials in the presence of various organic solvents was evaluated by adding 100 μL of 0.5 mg/mL solutions of glycerol, ethylene glycol, ethanol, hexadecanol, and 1,2-hexanediol to the reaction, respectively. Relative activity was determined by comparing the results with those obtained without the addition of inorganic salts, surfactants, and organic solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The determination of PPD and OPD in hair dyes
ce:section.ce:para: The chromogenic reaction of PPD and OPD is catalyzed by CuCDs. PPD was used as the representative for temperature optimization. To investigate the optimal reaction temperature, 100 μL of 100 mM PPD and 100 μL of 1 mg/mL CuCDs were added to 800 μL of HEPES buffer (pH 7.0, 0.1 M). The mixture was then incubated at 30–60 °C for 20 min and the corresponding absorbances were measured at 520 nm. For the quantitative analysis, different amounts of PPD or OPD were added to the 800 μL HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.0) containing 100 μL 1.0 mg/mL CuCDs and reacted at 50 °C for 20 min to determine their absorbance. The limit of detection was calculated as 3α/K, where α represents the standard deviation of the measured blank sample and K is the slope of the linear equation [22].

For real sample analysis, about 0.1 g of hair dye sample was weighed and added to 4 mL of methanol. The mixture was subjected to ultrasound treatment for 15 min, followed by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 10 min to remove the residues. The supernatant was filtered with 0.45 μm microporous filter, and the filtrate was used as the sample solution for quantitative analysis [23].

Hair dyeing process: Hair samples approximately 3 cm in length were washed with 8 % shampoo and then immersed at room temperature in a solution containing 5 mg/mL of DA, 7 mg/mL of SDBS, and 100 μg/mL of CuCDs. The volume of the solution was chosen to ensure complete immersion of the hair samples. After 20 min, the hair samples were rinsed five times with water. Photographs of the treated hair were obtained by iPhone 13, and subsequent color analysis was conducted using Photoshop software. To evaluate color persistence after multiple washings, the samples were subjected to 5, 7, 10, 13 and 15 washes, respectively.

SEM of hair samples: Hair samples were pressed onto carbon tape and affixed to aluminum scanning electron microscope (SEM) stubs. The samples were then coated with 10 nm of gold and imaged using a Hitachi SU8030 cold field emission gun (cFEG) SEM at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV.

Preparation of hair cross-sections for optical microscopy: Hair samples were embedded in optimal cutting temperature compound, and 10 μm sections were cryosectioned at −20 °C and mounted onto glass microscope slides.

Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was combined with a live-dead assay to evaluate the growth and survival of human immortalized keratinocytes (HaCaT) and human dermal fibroblasts (HDF) cells in the presence of CuCDs. HaCaT or HDF cells were cultured in 96-well plates with varying concentrations of CuCDs (0 μg/mL, 0.1 μg/mL, 1 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL) in DMEM/high glucose supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin. The cultures were maintained at 37 °C in a humidified incubator with 5 % CO2 for 24 h. The cells were then subjected to three washes with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to remove free CuCDs. A total of 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well, followed by the addition of 90 μL of DMEM. The mixture was then incubated at 37 °C for an additional 4 h. Cell viability was evaluated by measuring the absorbance at 450 nm. For the calcein AM assay, HaCaT or HDF cells were rinsed once with PBS and incubated with 10 μM calcein AM dye (Molecular Probes, Eugene, OR) at room temperature for 20 min. Fluorescence was determined using an excitation/emission filter set of 485/538 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CuCDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: CuCDs were prepared using a one-step calcining method, with amaranth as the carbon source, copper sulfate as the metal source, and ammonium chloride as a molten salt. The morphology and size of the CuCDs were investigated by TEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. 1 A–B, the CuCDs are nearly monodispersed ellipsoidal crystals with an average dimension of 3.4 nm and their height is approximately 1∼3 nm. The XRD patterns (Fig. 1C) reveal a broad peak centered around 25°, which is attributed to the presence of highly disordered carbon atoms [24]. In Fig. S1A, the Raman spectrum displays a crystalline G band at 1583 cm−1 and a disordered D band at 1354 cm−1, which correspond to graphitized sp2 carbon and disordered sp3 carbon, respectively. The intensity ratio IG/ID is 1.2, suggesting that the CuCDs exhibit a high degree of graphitization [25].

The composition of the CuCDs was studied by UV–vis, FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 1D, the characteristic absorption peak of the CuCDs near 344 nm results from copper doping [26]. The characteristic peak of the azo bond at 522 nm present in the precursor is absent in the CuCDs, indicating that the azo bond was cleaved during the reaction [27]. To further confirm copper doping, the FT-IR spectra of amaranth, CDs, and CuCDs are compared. As illustrated in Fig. 1E, the characteristic peak at 1050 cm−1, assigned to the N–Cu bond, is observed only in the CuCDs, indicating the successful incorporation of copper into the particles [28]. The peaks at 3456 cm−1 and 1606 cm−1 are assigned to the ν(O–H)/ν(N–H) and ν(CO)/ν(CN), respectively. The functional groups and elements in the CuCDs were further investigated using XPS. As depicted in Fig. 1F and Table S1, the CuCDs are composed of 77.7 % C, 14.5 % O, and 4.8 % S, with trace amounts of Cu at 0.52 %, Cl at 0.68 % and N at 1.8 %. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum displays three peaks at 284.7 eV for CC/C–C, 286.3 eV for CN/C–O, and 288.7 eV for C–N/CO (Fig. S1B) [29]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum is characterized by three peaks at 530.8 eV, 532.0 eV and 534.0 eV, which can be attributed to OC, O–C and C–OH bonds, respectively (Fig. S1C) [30]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum exhibits two fitted peaks at 399.5 eV and 400.7 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, respectively (Fig. S1D). These results suggest that the cleavage of the azo bond during the carbonation of the precursor facilitates the incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the conjugated ring of the CuCDs [31]. In the high-resolution XPS spectra of copper (Fig. 1G), the peaks at 942.0 eV and 963.0 eV correspond to the Cu 2p 3/2 and Cu 2p 1/2 electrons of Cu2+, respectively. The lower binding energies of 934.9 eV (2p 3/2) and 955.3 eV (2p 1/2) demonstrate the presence of Cu+ [32]. To further investigate the valence state of the Cu element in CuCDs, the Cu LMM Auger spectrum is shown in Fig. 1H. The peak fitting at 572.7 eV indicates the presence of Cu+, while the peak at 568.7 eV is attributed to Cu2+ [33]. These results indicate that CuCDs contain active copper similar to the active centers in laccase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Laccase-like activity and catalytic stability of CuCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The laccase-like activity of the CuCDs was investigated through the colorimetric reaction between 4-AP and 2,4-DP (Fig. 2 A). As depicted in Fig. 2B, colorless 2,4-DP was oxidized and reacted with 4-AP during incubation with CuCDs to produce a distinct UV–vis absorption signal at 505 nm. On the contrary, pristine CDs and copper ions show no notable activity to induce the colorimetric reaction under the same conditions. This comparison proves that the unique coordination microenvironment of copper ion in CuCDs is critical for exerting their excellent catalytic properties. As shown in Fig. 2C, the activity is highest at pH 7.0, and all subsequent assessments were performed at this pH.

The kinetic parameters were obtained in the presence of the CuCDs, 4-AP and varied concentrations of 2,4-DP. As shown in Fig. 2D and Table S2, the calculated maximum reaction rate constants (Vmax) and Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) for CuCDs are 0.2 mM and 7.26 × 10−3 mM s−1, respectively, which are superior to those of natural laccase (Vmax = 0.122 × 10−3 mM s−1, K
m = 0.41 mM). Compared to many other previously reported nanozymes and laccase (Fig. 2E–Table S2), the CuCDs nanozyme exhibits a higher Vmax, K cat/K m and a lower K m, indicating that CuCDs is an excellent alternative to natural laccase.

In addition, the stability of CuCDs was evaluated under different pH, temperatures, storage time, inorganic salt, surfactants, and organic solutions (Fig. 3 ). As shown in Fig. 3A, the CuCDs retains 88 % of its activity at pH 3.0 and 91 % at pH 9.0, while laccase loses approximately 30 % and 68 % of its activity at pH 3.0 and pH 9.0, respectively. Therefore, CuCDs serves as an effective catalyst over a wider pH range compared to laccase. The activity of laccase begins to decrease significantly at 20 °C and is almost completely inactivated above 50 °C. In contrast, the activity of CuCDs remains almost unchanged even at 60 °C (Fig. 3B). As for the effect of storage time, as shown in Fig. 3C, the CuCDs maintain 90 % of their activity after 30 days, whereas the activity of laccase decreases significantly after just 3 days, indicating the high stability of CuCDs. In practical applications, the presence of inorganic salts is often unavoidable. Therefore, we investigated the effects of various inorganic salts on the catalytic activity of CuCDs and laccase. As illustrated in Fig. 3D, F−, CO3 2−, and SO4 2− had no significant effect on the catalytic activity of CuCDs, while Cl− increases the catalytic activity of CuCDs by approximately 74 %. This enhancement is attributed to the catalytic facilitation of Cl− oxidation by O2 in the presence of CuCDs [38]. In contrast, laccase activity decreased in the presence of the selected inorganic salts. Additionally, the stability of enzyme mimics in the presence of surfactants was evaluated and compared to that of laccase (Fig. 3E). Interestingly, the performance of CuCDs was enhanced by approximately 70 % in the presence of SDBS compared to its absence, while the activity of CuCDs remained essentially unchanged in the presence of CAB, SDS, and DTAMC. The result is presumably due to the accumulation of hydrophobic 2,4-DP in the micellar hydrophobic cores formed by SDBS, which promotes the adsorption of 2,4-DP onto CuCDs, thereby facilitating the catalytic reaction [39]. To investigate the effect of organic solvents on catalytic activity, various organic solvents, including glycerol, ethylene glycol, ethanol, hexadecanol, and hexanediol, were mixed with the reactants. As shown in Fig. 3F, these organic solvents had no significant effect on the catalytic performance of CuCDs compared to natural laccase. The results of these stability tests show that CuCDs have significant stability and broad potential for practical applications.

In this study, a direct colorimetric detection method based on laccase mimics was employed to determine the levels of PPD and OPD. To optimize the reaction temperature, PPD was selected as a representative aniline compound. As shown in Fig. S2, the highest oxidation rate of PPD was observed at 50 °C, and all subsequent measurements were conducted at this temperature. In the presence of CuCDs, it was observed that the color of the PPD solution changed from colorless to brown, and the absorbance of the reaction system was directly dependent on the concentration of PPD (Fig. 4 A and inset). The limits of detection (LOD) and quantitation (LOQ) were determined using the following equations [22].
(2)
LOD = 3 α / K
(3)
LOQ = 10 α / K
Where α represents the standard deviation of the measured blank samples, and K denotes the slope of the calibration curve. The calculated LOD and LOQ values are presented in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 4C, after 20 min of reaction, a good linear relationship was established between the absorbance and the concentration of PPD in the range of 5.0–50.0 μM, with a LOD as low as 0.413 μM. Similarly, when OPD was used, the color of the reaction system changed from colorless to yellow (Fig. 4B and inset). Its absorbance at 415 nm exhibited a good linear relationship with the concentration of OPD in the range of 10.0–50.0 μM (Fig. 4D). The LOD for OPD was calculated to be 1.75 μM. Compared with other methods (Table S3), the proposed method has the advantages of higher sensitivity, low cost and simple operation. To evaluate the selectivity of the CuCDs assay for PPD and OPD determination, we tested some potential interferences, including m-phenylenediamine (MPD), resorcinol, 1-naphthol, and benzyl alcohol. As shown in Fig. S3, the addition of these substances up to 10 times had little influence on the color and the absorbance, indicating that the method exhibits good selectivity for the determination of OPD and PPD. To verify the validity of the colorimetric method, we employed the CuCDs-based colorimetric detection method to determine PPD and OPD in real hair dye samples and compared the results with those obtained by HPLC (Fig. S4). As shown in Table 1 , standard addition was also performed, and the recoveries of spiked samples ranged from 98.8 % to 105.3 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 2 %. Therefore, the chromogenic reaction can be used for the determination of PPD and OPD in real samples. To explore the reproducibility for OPD and PPD determinations, five groups of samples were measured separately under the identical experimental conditions and the results were found to be highly reproducible (Fig. S5). The RSD of all measured results was found to be within 1 %, indicating that the method has good reproducibility and can provide accurate determination capability. Figs. S6A–B show that the UV–vis absorption peaks of the products from the colorimetric reactions are consistent with the reported UV–vis spectra of Bandrowski's base and 2,3-diaminophenazine [40,41], suggesting that CuCDs can catalyze the conversion of PPD to compound Bandrowski's base and OPD to compound 2,3-diaminophenazine in the presence of oxygen (Fig. 5 ). The difference in peak wavelengths between the two compounds is approximately 100 nm, which provides a possibility for the selective determination of PPD and OPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CuCDs catalyzed DA polymerization for hair dyeing
ce:section.ce:para: CuCDs can catalyze not only aniline derivatives, such as OPD, but also phenolic derivatives. DA, a phenolic substance, can be polymerized under the catalysis of laccase. The effect of different processes on DA polymerization was studied and the corresponding results were analyzed by UV–vis. As illustrated in Fig. 6 A, the highest degree of DA polymerization was obtained at a concentration of 5 mg/mL, which was therefore selected as the optimal concentration for subsequent hair dyeing. As for the effect of pH, Fig. 6B shows that the best DA polymerization performances were observed at pH 7.0, which can be attributed to the highest catalytic activity of CuCDs under these conditions. By comparing with the control experiment, it was found that the polymerization rate of DA was significantly accelerated in the presence of CuCDs, indicating the crucial catalytic role of CuCDs as laccase-like mimics (Fig. S7). In different atmospheric conditions (N2, O2, and air), DA oxidation experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 6C, the polymerization degree of DA under an O2 atmosphere is significantly higher than that under air or N2, indicating that O2 plays a crucial role in the laccase-catalyzed reaction. Temperature is one of the main factors affecting the oxidation of DA. As shown in Fig. 6D, the reaction proceeds at the fastest rate at 40 °C, and the reaction rate decreases with increasing temperature, which is presumed to be due to the reduction in dissolved oxygen caused by the rise in temperature, thereby inhibiting the reaction. In addition, the influence of the SDBS concentration on the reaction was studied (Fig. 6E). The absorbance measured after 5min of reaction showed that the degree of polymerization of DA remained basically unchanged with the increase of SDBS concentration. Therefore, the concentration of SDBS was selected as 0.05 % (w/w).

The superiority of the optimized CuCDs/DA system was demonstrated through hair dyeing experiments using grey hair samples. As shown in Fig. 6F, DA polymerization catalyzed by CuCDs and other oxidants resulted in significant differences in hair color, and the corresponding color intensity was measured using Adobe Photoshop software (Fig. 6G). The hair samples treated with CuCDs showed the darkest black, with an L∗ value of 25. This coloring effect demonstrated strong color consistency when compared to the L∗ range (28–32) of the natural black hair of the young volunteers surveyed (Figs. S8A–B). Although laccase can catalyze DA for dyeing, the resulting color depth is insufficient.

To achieve the same staining effect as the CuCDs-catalyzed reaction, the reaction time of the laccase-catalyzed process needs to be increased by about 40 times [42]. In addition, the hair samples treated with Cu2+/H2O2 or NH4OH exhibited a grey color, with corresponding L∗ values exceeding 50, indicating the efficiency of DA oxidation in the presence of CuCDs.

The surface and cross-sectional morphology of the hair samples were examined using a SEM and an ultra-depth three-dimensional (3D) microscope, respectively (Fig. S9). The SEM images indicate that the hair surfaces after dyeing are coated with PDA, which is consistent with findings from previous reports [43]. Through optical microscopy analysis of hair cross-sections, the color remained localized on the outer layer. Compared to commercial hair dyes, PDA does not penetrate the hair shaft and has little effect on the hair structure, which is conducive to maintaining hair health [44]. The better improvement in the tensile properties of CuCDs-treated hair, relative to untreated grey hair, well supports this conclusion (Fig. S10).

The durability of the dyed hair samples was assessed through washing experiments. As shown in Fig. S11A, the PDA-coated hair samples basically retained their original color after fifteen shampoo washes, which was further confirmed by the corresponding color analysis (Fig. S11B). The excellent color fastness of PDA-coated hair could be attributed to the strong interactions between PDA and the hair surface. To investigate the interaction forces associated with PDA deposition, FT-IR was utilized to characterize the functional groups on the hair surface before and after dyeing. As shown in Fig. S12, the peak at 1004 cm−1 can be assigned to the C–H out-of-plane bending vibration of benzene ring in PDA, which was also present in the dyed hair [45]. The peak at 1122 cm−1 is attributed to the in-plane bending of the hydroxyl group, and this peak is observed in both dyed hair and PDA [46]. The peak at 1267 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C–N in the nitrogen-containing heterocyclic ring in the PDA [47]. Compared with untreated hair, this peak is observed in the dyed hair, indicating that PDA has been successfully coated on the hair surface. The FT-IR spectra of treated and untreated hair showed no significant changes, presumably due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between PDA and the hair.

CuCDs are used as an ingredient in hair dye formulations, and their cytotoxicity was evaluated in HaCaT and HDF cells. As shown in Fig. 7A–B and D- E, the results of the CCK-8 assay indicate that HaCaT and HDF cells treated with various concentrations of CuCDs maintained normal metabolic activity. Notably, cell viability did not decrease with increasing CuCDs concentrations, suggesting that CuCDs are essentially non-cytotoxic. The cytotoxicity of CuCDs was further assessed by live/dead cell viability assays. As shown in Fig. 7C and F, treatment of HaCaT and HDF cells with CuCDs had no significant detrimental effect on cell survival. Both cell death analysis and morphological examination revealed no signs of toxicity, suggesting that CuCDs are essentially non-toxic and safe as a hair dye ingredient.

To investigate the catalytic mechanism of CuCDs, various scavengers were used to identify reactive oxygen species during the oxidation process (Fig. S13A). Benzoquinone was used to quench •O2 −, furfural and histidine were used to quench 1O2. NaIO3 was employed to quench electrons [48]. Among these scavengers, benzoquinone, furfural, and histidine have little effect on the reaction process, while NaIO3 significantly inhibits the oxidation of 2,4-DP, suggesting that the reaction proceeds via electron transfer. As shown in Fig. S12B, the addition of EDTA-2Na to the reaction system to chelate copper ions in CuCDs can inhibit the catalytic activity of CuCDs, indicating that copper ions play a crucial role in the catalytic function of CuCDs. To exclude the possible production of H2O2 during the O2 reduction process, CuCDs/2,4-DP was incubated in an oxygen-containing solution for 1 h at room temperature (Fig. S14A). Subsequently, TMB and horseradish peroxidase were introduced to the system, and no significant color change was observed within 30 min. In contrast, when H2O2 was added to the mixture, the color of the reaction solution changed from colorless to green, and the UV–vis absorption peak at 654 nm was significantly enhanced, indicating that H2O2 was not generated in the CuCDs catalytic system (Fig. S14B).

To evaluate the order of electron transfer in the CuCDs/DA/O2 reaction system, the system was divided into two half-cells, with O2 in the anode half-cell and DA in the cathode half-cell, and a salt bridge was used to prevent direct contact between them (Fig. S15). A significant voltage increase was observed when the cathode electrode was modified with CuCDs, and this effect was further enhanced by O2 bubbling in the solution. In contrast, no voltage increase was detected when unmodified electrodes were used or when the CuCDs-modified electrode was placed on the anode side. Furthermore, the results of fluorescence spectroscopy showed that the fluorescence quenching effect of CuCDs was significantly higher with DA than with oxygen (Fig. S16). These results indicate that the reaction proceeds via a direct electron transfer pathway, with CuCDs preferentially activating DA, followed by the activation of oxygen.

Based on the above studies, the polymerization mechanism of DA is proposed (Fig. 8 ). Initially, the laccase substrates DA (I) is adsorbed on the surface of CuCDs and oxidized to generate intermediate II. The oxidation product II is converted into III by cyclization, which is subsequently oxidized in the presence of oxygen to produce intermediate IV [49]. The IV is further adsorbed on the surface of CuCDs and oxidized, generating compound V, which undergoes tautomerization to form compound VI. The resulting VI is polymerized through a free radical coupling reaction to form PDA. Meanwhile, the abundant hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups on the surface of CuCDs can enhance O2 adsorption and facilitate electron transfer in the reaction system [50]. Finally, O2 is reduced to H2O, completing the catalytic cycle.

In conclusion, we have designed and synthesized CuCDs with excellent laccase-like activity, almost non-toxicity, and stable enzyme activity over a wide range of pH values and temperatures, as well as during long-term storage. The mechanistic studies revealed that the catalytic process follows a classical electron transfer pathway, i.e., which first catalyzes laccase substrate and then reduces oxygen to water. This unique property enables CuCDs to be used as a colorimetric chemosensor for the determination of aniline-based pigments (OPD and PPD) in hair dyes under mild conditions. In addition, CuCDs can effectively catalyze the oxidative polymerization of DA to produce PDA for safe hair dyeing that avoids the use of toxic ingredients. Thus present work not only provides a detection method for the identification of harmful hair dyes but also expands the application of carbon nanomaterials in cosmetics.

Zhanghong Guo: Writing – original draft, Validation, Data curation. Lin Zhou: Formal analysis. Liping Zhang: Formal analysis. Haining Cui: Formal analysis. Jinxin Ma: Formal analysis. Chan Wang: Formal analysis. Qijun Song: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (22376081) and Key Program for International S&T Cooperation Projects of China (2022YEE0196700). We would like to acknowledge the work of Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2025.344071.
